| 'In fact it's about three million years old.<br/><br/>You are looking at the first proof<br/>of intelligent life beyond the Earth.'<br/>The speaker is Dr Roy Michaels, Chief Scientist at the Clavius Base on the<br/>Moon. The audience has been carefully chosen, because it is too soon to tell the<br/>world's population that they are not alone in the universe. On the screen behind Dr<br/>Michaels is a photograph of a black object about three metres high, of regular shape<br/>and with straight edges. It was certainly made by an intelligent form of life, and it<br/>was found buried under the surface of the Moon.<br/>If it is a message from another time, from a distant star, why has it been put<br/>there ? A possible answer comes soon afterwards, when the first light of the sun touches<br/>the object. It then sends out a powerful radio signal, aimed exactly at Saturn. As one of<br/>the scientists says,'You hide a sun-powered object in darkness — only if you want to<br/>know when it is brought out into the light.' So, far out in space, there may be intelligent<br/>beings who now know that men and women have taken their first steps away from<br/>Earth.<br/>This book is a journey. We watch as people move forward from their early<br/>beginnings into the future, and as one man, on the space ship Discovery, travels a billion<br/>kilometres from Earth to make contact again among the rings of Saturn.<br/>In 1964, before men had even landed on the Moon, the film director Stanley<br/>Kubrick was looking for a story for a science fiction film. He asked Arthur C. Clarke<br/>for help, and the two men worked together on the development of the plot. Clarke<br/>wrote the novel (which came out in 1968), while Kubrick made the film, and both<br/>became extremely famous.<br/><br/>PART ONE Ancient Night<br/>Chapter 1 The Road to Death<br/>Very little rain had fallen for a long time, for at least ten million years. Here, in the<br/>place that one day would be called Africa, the man-apes of the grasslands were fighting a<br/>battle to stay alive, and they were not winning.<br/>About fifty of them lived in caves on the side of a small dry valley. There<br/>was a stream running down the middle. If the weather was very hot, its water dried<br/>up and the man-apes were thirsty.<br/>When the first light of morning came into the cave, Moon-Watcher saw<br/>that his father had died in the night. He did not know that the Old One was his father,<br/>because he did not know what a father was. But he felt a little anxious as he dragged (he<br/>dead body out of the cave. Outside, he did something that no other animal in the world<br/>could do — he stood up.<br/>Moon-Watcher was bigger than the others in his group. He was nearly a<br/>meter and a half high, though very thin because of I he constant hunger. His hairy<br/>body was half-way between ape and man, but his head looked quite human. His<br/>forehead was low, but there were signs of intelligence in his eyes.<br/>As he walked down the slope, the rest of the group saw him and began to come<br/>out of their caves. They moved towards the stream for their morning drink. MoonWatcher walked on until he found a small bush. He left the body there, knowing that<br/>animals would do the rest. He never thought of his father again.<br/>His two females, the adults from the other cave, and most of the young ones<br/>were looking for berries among the small trees further up the valley. Only the babies<br/>and the very old were left<br/>In the caves. If there was any extra food at the end of the day, they might be fed.<br/>If not, they would stay hungry.<br/>Moon-Watcher climbed the slope to join the group. After some time he found<br/>honey in a dead tree. This did not happen very often, and the group was happy. Of<br/>course, they also collected a number of bee stings, but they hardly noticed these. Now,<br/>although Moon-Watcher was still hungry, he was not actually weak with hunger. He<br/>could not expect more than that.<br/>He led his group back to the stream. The others were there, as usual. There were<br/>about thirty of them, and they looked exactly the same as Moon-Watcher's own<br/>group. As they saw him coming, they began to dance, shake their arms and shout, and<br/>his people did the same.<br/>And that was all that happened. Although the man-apes sometimes fought<br/>among themselves, they could do little harm to each other. Their teeth were not<br/>sharp and their bodies were protected by thick hair. Also, they did not often have<br/>enough energy for fighting. After a time, the man-apes on both sides grew quiet and<br/>began to drink the muddy water.<br/>1<br/><br/>On the grassland near the caves there were many animals, but the man-apes<br/>knew of no way to kill one of them. In fact, they could not even imagine the idea of<br/>killing one. In the middle of so much food, they were slowly dying of hunger.<br/>That night a cold wind blew. Moon-Watcher hardly moved when the screams<br/>came from one of the lower caves. He knew what was happening before he heard the<br/>sound of the leopard. But Moon-Watcher did not think of going to help. He lay<br/>quietly, as all the others did.<br/>Later, he went outside and sat on a rock. He looked up and down the valley,<br/>then at the Moon. The man-apes were the only animal that ever did this. MoonWatcher had done it since his childhood. He was old now, twenty-five years old. If<br/>he was lucky, he might live another ten years.<br/>He stayed on the rock for some time, sleeping and waking but always listening.<br/>If any animal moved in the area, Moon-Watcher would know about it. But he did not<br/>see the bright light, brighter than any star that crossed the sky twice, rising high and<br/>sinking down to the east.<br/>Chapter 2<br/><br/>The New Rock<br/><br/>Late that night Moon-Watcher suddenly woke up. He sat up in the darkness of<br/>the cave, looking out into the night, and fear entered his soul. He had never heard a<br/>sound like this in his life. The big cats approached in silence, except perhaps for the<br/>occasional breaking of a stick or a fall of earth. But this was a continuous crashing noise<br/>that grew steadily louder. Even the elephants did not make as much noise as this.<br/>Then he heard a sound which he could not understand, because it had never<br/>been heard before in the history of the world. It was the sound of metal hitting stone.<br/>Moon-Watcher saw the New Rock when he led the group down to the river<br/>in the first light of morning. He had almost forgotten his fear during the night, and<br/>did not connect this strange thing with it.<br/>It was three times his height, but when he reached out his hands to each side,<br/>it was not as wide. When he walked round and looked at it from the side, it was quite<br/>thin. And he could see right through it. In fact, he only knew it was there when the<br/>sunlight flashed on its edges. He put out his hand and touched its cold surface.<br/>It was a rock, of course, and it had grown during the night. He knew of small<br/>round plants that grew during the night and looked a little like rocks. These tasted<br/>good, so Moon-Watcher put out his tongue and tasted the new rock. A few seconds<br/>were enough to make him understand that this thing was not food. So he continued on<br/>his way to the river and soon forgot all about it.<br/>In the last light of day, as the group climbed back towards the caves, the sound<br/>began. It was low and continuous. It came from the new rock, and they all stopped to<br/>listen. Then they turned and, like sleep-walkers, began to move towards the rock. They<br/>sat in a circle round the strange thing as the darkness came down and a light grew<br/><br/>inside it.<br/>The noise grew louder and lights began to flash and move, linking patterns<br/>that changed all the time. The man-apes watc he d, their mouths open, not<br/>knowing that the thi ng wa s e xa mining their mi nds .<br/>Suddenly one of them stood up. He picked up a piece of grass and tried to tie it<br/>into a knot. His eyes were wide with terror as he struggled to make his fingers do what<br/>they had never done before. He failed. The broken pieces of grass fell to the ground<br/>and he froze into stillness again.<br/>Another man-ape began to move. This one was younger and after some time it<br/>succeeded. On the planet Earth the first knot had been tied.<br/>Others did stranger things. Some held their hands out and tried to touch their<br/>fingertips together. Some were made to stare at patterns in the rock, watching lines<br/>which became thinner and thinner until they could not be seen.<br/>Moon-Watcher stood and picked up a stone. Now on the new rock was a series<br/>of circles, each one smaller than the next. The smallest one was black inside. Obeying<br/>the instructions in his brain, he threw the stone. He had never done this before, and he<br/>missed by some distance.<br/>Try again, said the command. He found another stone and threw. It hit the<br/>edge of the rock and made a ringing sound. His third throw hit the circles, only<br/>centimeters from the central black one. A strong feeling of pleasure passed through<br/>his body.<br/>One by one, every member of the group was tested and then al lowed to feel<br/>either pleasure or pain. Then the light in the rock died away. Shaking their heads, the<br/>man-apes got up and began to walk along the path that led to their caves. They did<br/>not look back at the strange light that was showing them the way to their homes — and<br/>to an unknown future, perhaps even to the stars.<br/>Chapter 3<br/><br/>Education<br/><br/>In the morning Moon-Watcher and his group had no memories of what had<br/>happened the night before. Här, på den plats som en dag skulle kallas Afrika, kämpade grässlätternas människoapor en strid för att hålla sig vid liv, och de vann inte.<br/>Ett femtiotal av dem bodde i grottor på sidan av en liten torr dal. Det var en ström som rann ner i mitten. Om vädret var mycket varmt, torkade dess vatten upp och människoaporna var törstiga.<br/>När det första morgonljuset kom in i grottan, såg Moon-Watcher att hans far hade dött i natten. Han visste inte att den gamle var hans far, eftersom han inte visste vad en far var. Men han kände sig litet ängslig, då han släpade (han död kropp ut ur grottan. utanför gjorde han något, som inget annat djur i världen kunde göra<unk> han reste sig upp.<br/>Moon-Watcher var större än de andra i hans grupp. Han var nästan en och en halv meter hög, men mycket tunn på grund av jag han konstant hunger. Hans håriga kropp var halvvägs mellan apa och människa, men hans huvud såg ganska mänsklig. Hans panna var låg, men det fanns tecken på intelligens i hans ögon.<br/>När han gick nerför sluttningen, såg resten av gruppen honom och började komma ut ur sina grottor. De flyttade mot strömmen för sin morgondrink. MoonWatcher gick vidare tills han hittade en liten buske. Han lämnade kroppen där, i vetskap om att djuren skulle göra resten.<br/>Hans två honor, de vuxna från den andra grottan och de flesta av ungarna letade efter bär bland de små träden längre upp i dalen. Endast ungarna och de mycket gamla var kvar<br/>Om det fanns extra mat i grottorna, skulle de kanske få mat.<br/>Om inte, skulle de vara hungriga.<br/>Moon-Watcher klättrade uppför sluttningen för att gå med i gruppen. Efter en tid hittade han honung i ett dött träd. Detta hände inte så ofta, och gruppen var glad. Naturligtvis samlade de också ett antal bistick, men de märkte knappt dessa. Fastän Moon-Watcher fortfarande var hungrig, var han inte svag av hunger, och han kunde inte förvänta sig mer än så.<br/>Han ledde sin grupp tillbaka till strömmen. De andra var där, som vanligt. Det var ungefär trettio av dem, och de såg exakt ut som Moon-Watchers egen grupp. När de såg honom komma, började de dansa, skaka armar och skrika, och hans folk gjorde detsamma.<br/>Och det var allt som hände. Även om människoaporna ibland kämpade sinsemellan, kunde de inte göra någon skada på varandra. Deras tänder var inte vassa och deras kroppar skyddades av tjockt hår. De hade inte heller ofta tillräckligt med energi för att slåss. Efter en tid blev man-aporna på båda sidor tysta och började dricka det leriga vattnet.<br/>1<br/><br/>På gräsmarken nära grottorna fanns det många djur, men människoaporna visste inget sätt att döda en av dem. I själva verket kunde de inte ens föreställa sig tanken på att döda en. I mitten av så mycket mat, de långsamt dör av hunger.<br/>Den natten blåste en kall vind. Månväktaren rörde sig knappt när skriken kom från en av de lägre grottorna. Han visste vad som hände innan han hörde leopardens ljud. Men Månväktaren tänkte inte på att gå för att hjälpa. Han låg tyst, som alla de andra.<br/>Senare gick han ut och satte sig på en klippa. Han tittade upp och ner i dalen, sedan på månen. Mannapor var det enda djuret som någonsin gjorde detta. MoonWatcher hade gjort det sedan sin barndom. Han var gammal nu, tjugofem år gammal. Om han hade tur skulle han leva i tio år till.<br/>Han stannade på klippan en tid, sov och vaknade men lyssnade alltid.<br/>Om något djur rörde sig i området, skulle Moon-Watcher veta om det. Men han såg inte det starka ljuset, ljusare än någon stjärna som korsade himlen två gånger, stiger högt och sjunker ner mot öster.<br/>Kapitel 2<br/><br/>Den nya rocken<br/><br/>Sent den natten vaknade Månväktaren plötsligt. Han satte sig upp i grottans mörker och såg ut i natten, och fruktan trängde in i hans själ. Han hade aldrig hört ett sådant ljud i sitt liv. De stora katterna närmade sig under tystnad, utom kanske för att ibland bryta en pinne eller ett jordfall. Men detta var ett kontinuerligt kraschande ljud som växte stadigt högre. Inte ens elefanterna gjorde så mycket ljud som detta.<br/>Då hörde han ett ljud, som han icke kunde förstå, ty det hade aldrig hörts förut i världshistorien; det var ljudet av metall som slog i sten.<br/>Moon-Watcher såg den nya klippan när han ledde gruppen ner till floden i det första ljuset på morgonen. Han hade nästan glömt sin rädsla under natten och kopplade inte denna konstiga sak med den.<br/>Den var tre gånger hans höjd, men när han sträckte ut händerna åt vardera sidan, var den inte lika bred. När han gick runt och såg på den från sidan, var den ganska tunn. Och han kunde se rakt igenom den. Han visste att den fanns där när solljuset blixtrade mot dess kanter, och han räckte ut handen och rörde vid dess kalla yta.<br/>Det var en sten, naturligtvis, och den hade vuxit under natten. Han kände till små runda växter som växte under natten och såg lite ut som stenar. Dessa smakade bra, så Moon-Watcher släckte ut sin tunga och smakade den nya stenen. Några sekunder räckte för att få honom att förstå, att den här saken inte var mat, så han fortsatte på väg till floden och glömde snart allt om den.<br/>I det sista ljuset på dagen, när gruppen klättrade tillbaka mot grottorna, började ljudet. Det var lågt och kontinuerligt. Det kom från den nya klippan, och de stannade alla för att lyssna. Sedan vände de och som sömngångare började de röra sig mot klippan.
